connection.note: extract-based - original logical source was 'excel'; actual rows come from the packaged .hyper file, not a live connection

fields.caption: Delay KPI
fields.tableau_formula:
If Avg([Shipment Delay (Hrs)])<1 then "GOOD" elseif
Avg([Shipment Delay (Hrs)])<10 then "OK" else
"BAD" end

fields.caption: Profitability KPI
fields.tableau_formula:
If Avg([Calculation_4760317224722252])>0.2 then "GOOD" elseif
Avg([Calculation_4760317224722252])>0.1 then "OK" else
"BAD" end

fields.caption: Not Yet Shipped
fields.tableau_formula:
(datediff('day',Today(),[Actual Ship Date]) >= 1
AND
datediff('year',Today(),[Actual Ship Date]) = 0)
OR ISNULL([Actual Ship Date])

fields.caption: Shipment Delay (Hrs, delays only)
fields.tableau_formula:
IF datediff('second',[Actual Ship Date],[Expected Ship Date])/3600
< 0 THEN 0 ELSE
datediff('second',[Actual Ship Date],[Expected Ship Date])/3600
END

theme.source_note: Tableau has no single global theme file - aggregated from per-worksheet mark-color formats; treat as a starting palette, not an authoritative theme to clone.

limitations_encountered.issue: extract-based (.hyper) data source - row data requires a separate extraction step (tableauhyperapi -> Parquet) or repointing to the true upstream system
LOD expression - verify DAX translation grain/filter-context against a known Tableau value

Materialization decision: extract-based (.hyper) source has no live connection, so the semantic model imports from the pre-materialized CSV (data/ds.sheet1_shippingdatafinal_xls.csv, 5510 rows) via an M 'SourceFolder' parameter holding the absolute data-folder path. To repoint at a real upstream system, change only the SourceFolder parameter (or replace the partition M query). Clearly-labeled sample-vs-production status: this is the true extracted data, not a stub.

The parser's fields[] omitted 3 physical columns present in the materialized extract (Billable Miles, Expected Ship Date, Status) - always cross-check the CSV header against fields[] for extract-only sources. All 3 were modeled as real columns (Billable Miles is required by the Billable Rate/Profit Per Mile measures and the Shipment Delay calc needs Expected Ship Date).

FIXED LOD resolved as a DAX calculated column using DIVIDE(CALCULATE(SUM(Profit),ALLEXCEPT('Shipments','Shipments'[Shipment ID])),CALCULATE(SUM('Shipments'[Pay Amount]),ALLEXCEPT(...))). Shipment ID is NOT unique (5508 distinct / 5510 rows; ID 999220 has 3 rows summing Profit=150, Pay=840 -> ratio 0.178571), confirming the LOD genuinely aggregates; a calc column (not AVERAGEX over distinct IDs) preserves Tableau's per-row replication weighting. Overall AVG ratio replicated in Python = 0.190199. Resolves the parse-stage high-severity LOD flag.

POSSIBLE SOURCE-WORKBOOK BUG (translated faithfully, not 'fixed'): Tableau formula is datediff('second',[Actual Ship Date],[Expected Ship Date])/3600 = Expected - Actual, so LATE shipments yield NEGATIVE delay and the Delay KPI rates very-early shipments as BAD. Modeled as ('Shipments'[Expected Ship Date] - 'Shipments'[Actual Ship Date]) * 24 (identical for whole-second timestamps; avoids DATEDIFF sign ambiguity) with null-guards to match Tableau null propagation. Flag argument order back to the customer.

Excluded from the model: Tableau 'Measure Names' pseudo-dimension is not real data. The 3 Measure Values virtual pivots (profitability_details, profitability_by_company, sheet_5) are handled at report-build time by binding each resolved pivoted_field_id directly onto the target visual (see the corresponding parse-stage limitations).

Excluded from the model: Tableau ad-hoc group with only a partial 6-state mapping surfaced, NOT materialized in the extract, and referenced by no worksheet. If a grouped-geography rollup is later needed, add it as a calculated column or a mapping table at report time.

No gauge/KPI reference-line measures were created: all 7 worksheets have reference_lines: [] (empty). The '<base> Min'/'Max'/'Target' suffix-naming convention was reserved but is unused for this workbook.

Money columns (Bill Amount, Pay Amount, Profit) modeled as Decimal (Currency.Type / fixed decimal), not Double, per modeling guidelines. Verified immaterial: replicating Profit Ratio per shipment with 4-decimal fixed-decimal emulation in Python gave the identical overall AVG (0.190199) as full double precision, so fixed-decimal typing introduces no drift at this data's scale.

Deliberate BPA deviation ('Hide fact table columns' flags Bill Amount, Billable Miles, Profit, Profit Ratio per shipment): base numeric columns kept VISIBLE with summarizeBy=sum so the report builder can implicitly aggregate them exactly as Tableau exposed them as draggable measures, avoiding orphan explicit measures and column/measure name collisions. Revisit if a stricter hidden-columns + explicit-measures posture is desired.

BPA 'Model should have a date table' not addressed by design: this is a faithful 1:1 migration of a single flat extract that had no date dimension in Tableau. A dedicated date table + time-intelligence is a recommended later report-modeling enhancement, not required for migration fidelity.

Data-quality finding (validates the DIVIDE guard): 67 of 5508 distinct Shipment IDs have SUM(Pay Amount)=0, so the FIXED-LOD 'Profit Ratio per shipment' calc column returns BLANK (via DAX DIVIDE) for those rows and AVERAGE excludes them - the faithful overall = 0.190199. A naive '/' would have produced infinity/errors, so the DIVIDE(...) choice is load-bearing. Report builder should expect blank profit-ratio cells for these shipments; consider flagging the zero-pay shipments back to the customer as a possible source-data issue.

Tableau's 'Profitability by Company' left column is a %-of-Total table calculation (each company's profit as a share of grand-total profit). This is a table-scoped running calc with no equivalent in the frozen semantic model (no %-of-grand-total measure) and no documented PBIR 'show value as % of grand total' quick-calc. Bound faithfully as SUM(Profit) with an honest 'Profit' ($) header instead. Row ranking, sort order, and KPI (GOOD/OK/BAD) row coloring are identical to the reference; only the left column's unit differs ($ vs % of total).

Column display formats come from the frozen model formatStrings (0.00% for ratio/discount, $#,##0.00 for amounts, #,##0.00 for delay hrs), so values render with 2 decimals and $ signs. The Tableau reference shows lower-precision, internally-inconsistent formats (integer % e.g. '12%', 1-decimal delay e.g. '-0.5', and no-$ on some amount columns). Verified the underlying value scale is correct (ratio is a true 0-1 fraction -> 0.00% renders the right value, just with more decimals). Purely cosmetic precision difference; not corrected to avoid overriding the authoritative model format strings.

Tableau dashboard filter actions ([Action (Customer)] / 'Click to Filter' on the company and detail tables) are translated to Power BI's default visual cross-highlight/cross-filter interactions between visuals on the same page, rather than explicit action objects. Click-to-filter behavior is preserved functionally; there is no 1:1 named-action artifact in PBIR.

Worksheet customized tooltip text is reproduced via the visuals' default field tooltips (the projected measures/dimensions appear on hover). Tableau's rich/dynamic tooltip layout and any inline formatting are not fully carried over. Consider a dedicated PBI tooltip page if exact tooltip fidelity is later required.

The page-2 'Delay Overview' scatter (Shipment Delay Hrs vs Pay Amount) has a fixed X-axis range so the dense main cluster stays legible despite a ~2906-hr extreme outlier. Tableau auto-ranges but visually the outlier is similarly off to the side; the fixed range is a faithful, more-legible rendering rather than a data change. All points remain bound; none are filtered out.

The 'Profitability Details' and 'Sheet 5 / Delay Details' worksheets use Tableau's Measure Names / Measure Values virtual pivot (with [Multiple Values] and Measure Names shelf refs that the parser surfaces as UNRESOLVED). These have no PBI equivalent and are translated by binding each underlying field directly to the table (one field-well entry per resolved measure, per pivoted_field_ids) rather than recreating the literal pivot column. No fields were silently dropped.
